content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS of length NNN.

He can perform the following operation on string SSS:


Select a substring of size MMM and flip all the characters of the selected substring, i.e., change 000 to 111 and 111 to 000.
There are QQQ queries of the following 222 types:


000: Append 000 to the front of string SSS.
111: Append 111 to the front of string SSS.
After each query, find the **minimum** number of operations required to make lexicographically smallest possible string from SSS. Note that queries are **dependent**. Thus, after each query, the string SSS gets updated.

**Note**: The input of this problem is large, so use fast input/output methods.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains three integers NNN, MMM, and QQQ.
The second line of each test case contains the string SSS.
After that, QQQ lines follow. The ithi^{th}ith line contains an integer XXX (0≤X≤1)( 0\leq X\leq 1)(0≤X≤1) - the ithi^{th}ith query. For each query, you need to append XXX to the front of string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, print QQQ lines. The ithi^{th}ith line should contain the answer to the ithi^{th}ith query.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.
Sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: Initially, S=10011S = 10011S=10011.


After the first query, SSS becomes 110011110011110011. The lexicographically minimum string into which SSS can be converted is 000000000000000000. The string 110011110011110011 can be converted to 000000000000000000 using 222 operations:

Operation 111: Select the subarray [1,2][1,2][1,2] and flip it. Now, SSS becomes 000011000011000011.
Operation 222: Select the subarray [5,6][5,6][5,6] and flip it. Now, SSS becomes 000000000000000000.

It can be proven that 110011110011110011 cannot be converted into 000000000000000000 in less than 222 operations.


After the second query, SSS becomes 011001101100110110011. The lexicographically minimum string into which SSS can be converted is 000000000000000000000. The string 011001101100110110011 can be converted to 000000000000000000000 using 222 operations:

Operation 111: Select the subarray [2,3][2,3][2,3] and flip it. Now, SSS becomes 000001100000110000011.
Operation 222: Select the subarray [6,7][6,7][6,7] and flip it. Now, SSS becomes 000000000000000000000.

It can be proven that 011001101100110110011 cannot be converted into 000000000000000000000 in less than 222 operations.


After the third query, SSS becomes 101100111011001110110011. The lexicographically minimum string into which SSS can be converted is 000000010000000100000001. The string 101100111011001110110011 can be converted to 000000010000000100000001 using 555 operations: These operations are [1,2][1,2][1,2], [2,3][2,3][2,3], [4,5][4,5][4,5], [5,6][5,6][5,6], and [6,7][6,7][6,7] in the given order.
It can be proven that SSS cannot be converted into a lexicographically smaller string than 000000010000000100000001 using any number of operations.
Also, it can be proven that 101100111011001110110011 cannot be converted into 000000010000000100000001 in less than 555 operations.
